result.title: Verify extracted snippets and prepare final cited answers

details.intent: After the visit returns the page content and extracted snippets, verify that each extracted fact (year, creator, stable version) is supported by the captured text and prepare the final answer payload with precise citations (text snippets + location).
verification_checks: Check that the 'first_released_year' appears as a four-digit year in the captured snippet and that the snippet clearly states it is the initial release year.
Check that the 'creator_name' appears exactly as a personal name in the snippet and is attributed as 'creator', 'designed by', or equivalent.
Check that the 'current_stable_version' snippet is a version string (contains digits and dots) and is labeled as 'Stable release', 'Latest release', or similar.
If any fact is ambiguous or stated differently in multiple places, prefer the infobox and lead paragraph; otherwise include both snippets and note discrepancy.
measurable_output_fields: citations (array of objects with fields: snippet_text, location_label, source_url)
details.rationale: This think node performs post-action validation required by the developer rules: reflect on the visit results, resolve ambiguities, and prepare the final structured answer with explicit citations drawn from the visited page.
details.goal: Verify extracted snippets and prepare final cited answers
details.original_goal: Verify extracted snippets and prepare final cited answers

You MUST visit the URL - do not rely on search results. Provide the information with citations from the actual page content.

6a5699fc-6539-443d-a583-1e66750bef14.title: Visit the Wikipedia page for Python (programming language) and extract requested facts

details.intent: Directly retrieve page content (HTML/text) and extract three facts with in-page citations: (1) year Python was first released, (2) name of Python's creator, (3) current stable version number.

target_facts.first_released_year: integer (e.g., 1991) — year of first/public release as stated on page
target_facts.creator_name: string — name of person credited as creator/designer on the page
target_facts.current_stable_version: string — version label as shown on the page (e.g., '3.x.y')

details.extraction_plan: 1) Load full page content. 2) Locate the infobox (right-hand summary box) and look for fields labeled 'First appeared', 'Initial release', 'First released', or similar to get the release year; copy the exact phrase/sentence and the surrounding text for citation. 3) In the infobox or lead paragraph, locate the creator/Designed by/Developer field to extract the creator's name; copy the exact phrase/sentence for citation. 4) Locate 'Stable release', 'Latest release', or 'Stable release' field in the infobox or the lead to extract the current stable version number; capture the exact snippet and any date associated. 5) If any of these are present in the article body rather than infobox, extract the sentence(s) and note the section heading. For each extracted fact capture: (a) the exact quoted text from the page, (b) the surrounding sentence for context, and (c) the page URL (this URL) as the citation. 6) Return measurable output fields (see below).
verification_steps: Prefer infobox fields because they are concise and authoritative on Wikipedia pages.
If infobox is absent or incomplete for a fact, search the lead section and the 'History' or 'Release history' sections for corroborating sentences.
Capture exact text and confirm the year/creator/version appear verbatim in the captured snippet.
Record the location (infobox vs. body) to make citation explicit and verifiable.
details.rationale: The user mandated a direct visit to the specific Wikipedia URL and requested citations from the actual page content. This visit node is required to fetch the authoritative text to extract verifiable facts rather than relying on search results or memory. The extraction plan targets the most reliable locations on the page (infobox, lead, and history sections).
details.note: A vector DB query will be auto-run for context on each node (per system rules) to check for related stored vectors; primary data source will be the visited page content.

details.original_goal: Visit the Wikipedia page for Python (programming language) and extract requested facts

You MUST visit the URL - do not rely on search results. Provide the information with citations from the actual page content.

86ed7662-1fce-42b4-b995-90f4bccea4fb.title: Verify extracted snippets and prepare final cited answers

details.intent: After the visit returns the page content and extracted snippets, verify that each extracted fact (year, creator, stable version) is supported by the captured text and prepare the final answer payload with precise citations (text snippets + location).
verification_checks: Check that the 'first_released_year' appears as a four-digit year in the captured snippet and that the snippet clearly states it is the initial release year.
Check that the 'creator_name' appears exactly as a personal name in the snippet and is attributed as 'creator', 'designed by', or equivalent.
Check that the 'current_stable_version' snippet is a version string (contains digits and dots) and is labeled as 'Stable release', 'Latest release', or similar.
If any fact is ambiguous or stated differently in multiple places, prefer the infobox and lead paragraph; otherwise include both snippets and note discrepancy.
measurable_output_fields: citations (array of objects with fields: snippet_text, location_label, source_url)
details.rationale: This think node performs post-action validation required by the developer rules: reflect on the visit results, resolve ambiguities, and prepare the final structured answer with explicit citations drawn from the visited page.
details.goal: Verify extracted snippets and prepare final cited answers
details.original_goal: Verify extracted snippets and prepare final cited answers

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a Christian
neo-Byzantine style
influenced by ninth-century architecture, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

details.intent: After the visit returns the raw link data and (when possible) lead-paragraph descriptions, format and organize the 10 links into a clear list: for each item show link URL, link text/title, and a 1-2 sentence description. Check completeness and prepare the final output.
target_facts: A neatly ordered array/list of 10 items where each item has: link_url, link_text/title, and a 1-2 sentence description.
verification_steps: Ensure there are exactly 10 items.
Ensure no duplicate URLs or link_texts.
Ensure each description is 1-2 sentences and references the source (lead paragraph or page summary).
If any descriptions are missing, annotate them and recommend follow-up visit nodes to fetch the lead paragraph.
details.rationale: This step consolidates raw extraction results into the user-requested organized format and performs final validation before returning results. A think node is appropriate here because it synthesizes and verifies data gathered by the preceding actions.

details.rationale: Tool rules require a search node to produce URLs that visit nodes consume. This search reduces uncertainty by confirming the exact URL to request and provides a verify-able snippet.

33d820de-84bd-44c3-8832-08b6b56adb32.title: Visit Wikipedia Main Page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page) and extract 10 links

details.intent: Load the full content of the Wikipedia main page and extract 10 distinct links with link URL and link text. For each link, obtain a concise (1-2 sentence) description of the linked page by extracting the first one or two sentences of that page's lead section (visit linked pages if needed).

items_per_element.description: string (1-2 sentences summarizing the linked page; source: first paragraph/lead section or page summary)

extraction_plan: 1) Parse the main page and identify candidate links in main content areas (e.g., Featured content, Today's featured article, In the news, Did you know, sidebar important links).
2) Select 10 prominent, unique links (prioritize links to article pages, not purely internal anchors or external sites).
3) For each selected link, capture the exact href (absolute URL) and anchor/link text.

4) For each selected link, attempt to retrieve the linked page's lead section (first paragraph). If the visit tool can follow links, fetch the linked page and extract the first 1-2 sentences. If not able to fetch linked pages in this single node, include the link URL and indicate that descriptions will be obtained by visiting each link in follow-up steps.
5) Ensure descriptions are 1-2 sentences, sourced from the linked page's lead paragraph or the summary text available on the main page if lead can't be obtained.
verification_steps: Confirm exactly 10 unique link_url entries were captured (no duplicates).
For each link, ensure link_text is non-empty and link_url is a valid http(s) URL.
Confirm each description is 1-2 sentences and cite which source (the linked page lead paragraph or main page summary).
If any link's description could not be retrieved, mark it explicitly and list the link for follow-up visits.
details.rationale: Visiting the main page is required to gather the list of links and their raw link text. The plan attempts to also gather short descriptions by visiting the linked pages (this yields verifiable, accurate summaries instead of guessing). This node reduces uncertainty by producing concrete URLs and the initial metadata needed to create any follow-up visits.

Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a Christian
neo-Byzantine style
influenced by ninth-century architecture, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.

details.intent: After the visit returns the raw link data and (when possible) lead-paragraph descriptions, format and organize the 10 links into a clear list: for each item show link URL, link text/title, and a 1-2 sentence description. Check completeness and prepare the final output.
target_facts: A neatly ordered array/list of 10 items where each item has: link_url, link_text/title, and a 1-2 sentence description.
verification_steps: Ensure there are exactly 10 items.
Ensure no duplicate URLs or link_texts.
Ensure each description is 1-2 sentences and references the source (lead paragraph or page summary).
If any descriptions are missing, annotate them and recommend follow-up visit nodes to fetch the lead paragraph.
details.rationale: This step consolidates raw extraction results into the user-requested organized format and performs final validation before returning results. A think node is appropriate here because it synthesizes and verifies data gathered by the preceding actions.

1) Page: Artificial intelligence
   - URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence
   - Page title / h1 (from visit result): "Artificial intelligence - Wikipedia" / "Artificial intelligence"
   - Why I chose the link: This is the required start page for the task.

See source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence
   - Note: The Artificial intelligence page's links list also contains a direct link to the target page https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing (present in the AI page links array), but I followed a two-step route via Computer science instead.

2) Page: Computer science
   - URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science
   - Page title / h1 (from visit result): "Computer science - Wikipedia" / "Computer science"
   - Why I chose the link: Computer science is a natural, broader discipline linking AI to computing topics; it was explicitly linked from the AI page and its page content lists subfields and adjacent fields relevant to quantum topics.
   - Outgoing link chosen (to next page / target): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing
   - Adjacency verification (evidence): the visit result for the Computer science page includes the link "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing" in its links list (merged result: "links" contains https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing). See source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science

3) Page: Quantum computing (target reached)
   - URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing
   - Page title / h1 (from visit result): "Quantum computing - Wikipedia" / "Quantum computing"
   - Why this is the target: This is the exact target URL requested in the mandate and the page visited in the final step.
   - Confirmation evidence: The visit result returned the page content and the page title "Quantum computing - Wikipedia". See source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing

Chain / URL order (explicit):
1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence -> 2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science -> 3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing

Adjacency verification summary (explicit statements linking each step):
- The Artificial intelligence page's extracted links list includes the Computer science URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science (so URL[1] contains link to URL[2]). Source: Artificial intelligence page visit result (links array). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence
- The Computer science page's extracted links list includes the Quantum computing URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing (so URL[2] contains link to URL[3]). Source: Computer science page visit result (links array). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science

Additional note (from provided evidence):
- The Artificial intelligence page's links list also directly contains the target URL https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_computing, so an alternate single-click path exists: Artificial intelligence -> Quantum computing. Source: Artificial intelligence page visit result (links array). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence

What I used from the provided evidence:
- The merged visit results for each page (Artificial intelligence, Computer science, Quantum computing), including the explicit links arrays that show which pages link to which URLs. Each adjacency claim above is supported by those extracted links lists in the merged results.

Missing evidence (if any):
- The merged results include the necessary link lists and page titles to document this path. No additional external evidence was required or used. If you want the exact anchor text used on the pages or the specific section headings where the links appear, that detail is not present in the provided merged results (the results give the links arrays but not the precise anchor/section context).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Study of computation
For other uses, see
Computer science (disambiguation)
.
Fundamental areas of computer science
Programming language theory
Computer architecture
Artificial intelligence
Computational complexity theory
Computer science
History
Outline
Glossary
Category
v
t
e
Computer science
is the study of
computation
,
information
, and
automation
.
[
1
]
[
2
]
[
3
]
Included broadly in
the sciences
, computer science spans
theoretical disciplines
(such as
algorithms
,
theory of computation
, and
information theory
) to
applied disciplines
(including the design and implementation of
hardware
and
software
).
[
4
]
[
5
]
[
6
]
An expert in the field is known as a
computer scientist
.
Algorithms and
data structures
are central to computer science.
[
7
]
The theory of computation concerns abstract
models of computation
and general classes of
problems
that can be solved using them. The fields of
cryptography
and
computer security
involve studying the means for secure communication and preventing
security vulnerabilities
.
Computer graphics
and
computational geometry
address the generation of images.
Programming language theory
considers different ways to describe computational processes, and
database
theory concerns the management of repositories of data.
Human–computer interaction
investigates the interfaces through which humans and computers interact, and software engineering focuses on the design and principles behind developing software. Areas such as
operating systems
,
networks
and
embedded systems
investigate the principles and design behind
complex systems
. Computer architecture describes the construction of computer components and computer-operated equipment.
Artificial intelligence
and
machine learning
aim to synthesize goal-orientated processes such as problem-solving, decision-making, environmental adaptation,
planning
and learning found in humans and animals. Within artificial intelligence,
computer vision
aims to understand and process image and video data, while
natural language processing
aims to understand and process textual and linguistic data.
The fundamental concern of computer science is determining what can and cannot be automated.
[
2
]
[
8
]
[
3
]
[
9
]
[
10
]
The
Turing Award
is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Study of computation
For other uses, see
Computer science (disambiguation)
.
Fundamental areas of computer science
Programming language theory
Computer architecture
Artificial intelligence
Computational complexity theory
Computer science
History
Outline
Glossary
Category
v
t
e
Computer science
is the study of
computation
,
information
, and
automation
.
[
1
]
[
2
]
[
3
]
Included broadly in
the sciences
, computer science spans
theoretical disciplines
(such as
algorithms
,
theory of computation
, and
information theory
) to
applied disciplines
(including the design and implementation of
hardware
and
software
).
[
4
]
[
5
]
[
6
]
An expert in the field is known as a
computer scientist
.
Algorithms and
data structures
are central to computer science.
[
7
]
The theory of computation concerns abstract
models of computation
and general classes of
problems
that can be solved using them. The fields of
cryptography
and
computer security
involve studying the means for secure communication and preventing
security vulnerabilities
.
Computer graphics
and
computational geometry
address the generation of images.
Programming language theory
considers different ways to describe computational processes, and
database
theory concerns the management of repositories of data.
Human–computer interaction
investigates the interfaces through which humans and computers interact, and software engineering focuses on the design and principles behind developing software. Areas such as
operating systems
,
networks
and
embedded systems
investigate the principles and design behind
complex systems
. Computer architecture describes the construction of computer components and computer-operated equipment.
Artificial intelligence
and
machine learning
aim to synthesize goal-orientated processes such as problem-solving, decision-making, environmental adaptation,
planning
and learning found in humans and animals. Within artificial intelligence,
computer vision
aims to understand and process image and video data, while
natural language processing
aims to understand and process textual and linguistic data.
The fundamental concern of computer science is determining what can and cannot be automated.
[
2
]
[
8
]
[
3
]
[
9
]
[
10
]
The
Turing Award
is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science.
[
11
]
[
12
]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Computer hardware technology that uses quantum mechanics
Layered Model of Quantum Computing
Bloch sphere
representation of a qubit. The state
|
ψ
⟩
=
α
|
0
⟩
+
β
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |\psi \rangle =\alpha |0\rangle +\beta |1\rangle }
is a point on the surface of the sphere, partway between the poles,
|
0
⟩
{\displaystyle |0\rangle }
and
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |1\rangle }
.
A
quantum computer
is a (real or theoretical)
computer
that exploits
superposed
and
entangled
states
. Quantum computers can be viewed as sampling from quantum systems. These systems evolve in ways that operate on an enormous number of possibilities simultaneously, though they remain subject to strict computational constraints. By contrast, ordinary ("classical") computers operate according to
deterministic
rules. (A
classical computer
can, in principle, be replicated by a
classical mechanical device
, with only a simple multiple of time cost. On the other hand (it is believed), a quantum computer would require
exponentially
more time and energy to be simulated classically.) It is widely believed that a quantum computer could perform
some
calculations exponentially faster than any classical computer. For example, a large-scale quantum computer could
break some widely used public-key cryptographic schemes
and aid physicists in performing
physical simulations
. However, current hardware implementations of quantum computation are largely experimental and only suitable for specialized tasks.
The basic
unit of information
in quantum computing, the
qubit
(or "quantum bit"), serves the same function as the
bit
in ordinary or "classical" computing.
[
1
]
However, unlike a classical bit, which can be in one of two states (a
binary
), a qubit can exist in a
linear combination
of two states known as a
quantum superposition
. The result of
measuring
a qubit is one of the two states given by a
probabilistic rule
. If a quantum computer manipulates the qubit in a particular way,
wave interference
effects amplify the probability of the desired measurement result. The design of
quantum algorithms
involves creating procedures that allow a quantum computer to perform this amplification.
Quantum computers are not yet practical for real-world applications. Physically engineering high-quality qubits has proven to be challenging. If a physical qubit is not sufficiently
isolated
from its environment, it suffers from
quantum decoherence
, introducing
noise
into calculations. National governments have invested heavily in experimental research aimed at developing scalable qubits with longer coherence times and lower error rates. Example implementations include
superconductors
(which isolate an
electrical current
by eliminating
electrical resistance
) and
ion traps
(which confine a single
atomic particle
using
electromagnetic fields
). Researchers have claimed, and are widely believed to be correct, that certain quantum devices can outperform classical computers on narrowly defined tasks, a milestone referred to as quantum advantage or
quantum supremacy
. These tasks are not necessarily useful for real-world applications.
History
[
edit
]
For a chronological guide, see
Timeline of quantum computing and communication
.
For many years, the fields of
quantum mechanics
and
computer science
formed distinct academic communities.
[
2
]
Modern quantum theory
was developed in the 1920s to explain perplexing physical phenomena observed at atomic scales,
[
3
]
[
4
]
and
digital computers
emerged in the following decades to replace
human computers
for tedious calculations.
[
5
]
Both disciplines had practical applications during
World War II
; computers played a major role in
wartime cryptography
,
[
6
]
and quantum physics

was developed in the 1920s to explain perplexing physical phenomena observed at atomic scales,
[
3
]
[
4
]
and
digital computers
emerged in the following decades to replace
human computers
for tedious calculations.
[
5
]
Both disciplines had practical applications during
World War II
; computers played a major role in
wartime cryptography
,
[
6
]
and quantum physics

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Study of computation
For other uses, see
Computer science (disambiguation)
.
Fundamental areas of computer science
Programming language theory
Computer architecture
Artificial intelligence
Computational complexity theory
Computer science
History
Outline
Glossary
Category
v
t
e
Computer science
is the study of
computation
,
information
, and
automation
.
[
1
]
[
2
]
[
3
]
Included broadly in
the sciences
, computer science spans
theoretical disciplines
(such as
algorithms
,
theory of computation
, and
information theory
) to
applied disciplines
(including the design and implementation of
hardware
and
software
).
[
4
]
[
5
]
[
6
]
An expert in the field is known as a
computer scientist
.
Algorithms and
data structures
are central to computer science.
[
7
]
The theory of computation concerns abstract
models of computation
and general classes of
problems
that can be solved using them. The fields of
cryptography
and
computer security
involve studying the means for secure communication and preventing
security vulnerabilities
.
Computer graphics
and
computational geometry
address the generation of images.
Programming language theory
considers different ways to describe computational processes, and
database
theory concerns the management of repositories of data.
Human–computer interaction
investigates the interfaces through which humans and computers interact, and software engineering focuses on the design and principles behind developing software. Areas such as
operating systems
,
networks
and
embedded systems
investigate the principles and design behind
complex systems
. Computer architecture describes the construction of computer components and computer-operated equipment.
Artificial intelligence
and
machine learning
aim to synthesize goal-orientated processes such as problem-solving, decision-making, environmental adaptation,
planning
and learning found in humans and animals. Within artificial intelligence,
computer vision
aims to understand and process image and video data, while
natural language processing
aims to understand and process textual and linguistic data.
The fundamental concern of computer science is determining what can and cannot be automated.
[
2
]
[
8
]
[
3
]
[
9
]
[
10
]
The
Turing Award
is generally recognized as the highest distinction in computer science.

11
]

12
]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Computer hardware technology that uses quantum mechanics
Layered Model of Quantum Computing
Bloch sphere
representation of a qubit. The state
|
ψ
⟩
=
α
|
0
⟩
+
β
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |\psi \rangle =\alpha |0\rangle +\beta |1\rangle }
is a point on the surface of the sphere, partway between the poles,
|
0
⟩
{\displaystyle |0\rangle }
and
|
1
⟩
{\displaystyle |1\rangle }
.
A
quantum computer
is a (real or theoretical)
computer
that exploits
superposed
and
entangled
states
. Quantum computers can be viewed as sampling from quantum systems. These systems evolve in ways that operate on an enormous number of possibilities simultaneously, though they remain subject to strict computational constraints. By contrast, ordinary ("classical") computers operate according to
deterministic
rules. (A
classical computer
can, in principle, be replicated by a
classical mechanical device
, with only a simple multiple of time cost. On the other hand (it is believed), a quantum computer would require
exponentially
more time and energy to be simulated classically.) It is widely believed that a quantum computer could perform
some
calculations exponentially faster than any classical computer. For example, a large-scale quantum computer could
break some widely used public-key cryptographic schemes
and aid physicists in performing
physical simulations
. However, current hardware implementations of quantum computation are largely experimental and only suitable for specialized tasks.
The basic
unit of information
in quantum computing, the
qubit
(or "quantum bit"), serves the same function as the
bit
in ordinary or "classical" computing.
[
1
]
However, unlike a classical bit, which can be in one of two states (a
binary
), a qubit can exist in a
linear combination
of two states known as a
quantum superposition
. The result of
measuring
a qubit is one of the two states given by a
probabilistic rule
. If a quantum computer manipulates the qubit in a particular way,
wave interference
effects amplify the probability of the desired measurement result. The design of
quantum algorithms
involves creating procedures that allow a quantum computer to perform this amplification.
Quantum computers are not yet practical for real-world applications. Physically engineering high-quality qubits has proven to be challenging. If a physical qubit is not sufficiently
isolated
from its environment, it suffers from
quantum decoherence
, introducing
noise
into calculations. National governments have invested heavily in experimental research aimed at developing scalable qubits with longer coherence times and lower error rates. Example implementations include
superconductors
(which isolate an
electrical current
by eliminating
electrical resistance
) and
ion traps
(which confine a single
atomic particle
using
electromagnetic fields
). Researchers have claimed, and are widely believed to be correct, that certain quantum devices can outperform classical computers on narrowly defined tasks, a milestone referred to as quantum advantage or
quantum supremacy
. These tasks are not necessarily useful for real-world applications.
History
[
edit
]
For a chronological guide, see
Timeline of quantum computing and communication
.
For many years, the fields of
quantum mechanics
and
computer science
formed distinct academic communities.
[
2
]
Modern quantum theory
was developed in the 1920s to explain perplexing physical phenomena observed at atomic scales,
[
3
]
[
4
]
and
digital computers
emerged in the following decades to replace
human computers
for tedious calculations.
[
5
]
Both disciplines had practical applications during
World War II
; computers played a major role in
wartime cryptography
,
[
6
]
and quantum physics

Produce a concise narrative and a verification block (page titles + canonical URLs + short evidence snippet).
details.expected_input: Results from the visit nodes including page_title, first_paragraph, and list_of_outgoing_links with exact URLs.
expected_output_fields: evidence_snippets (array of small text excerpts from each visited page confirming context)
details.rationale: A think step is required to synthesize the raw extraction outputs into the deliverable the user asked for: documented path, reasons for link choices, and demonstration that the Quantum computing page was reached.

reasons: The agent started at the required 'Artificial intelligence' page and ended at the required 'Quantum computing' page.
The path used a valid intermediate page ('Computer science') that is plausibly linked from the AI page and links to the Quantum computing page.
Link choices are explained (start page required, Computer science as a natural broader discipline connecting AI to computing topics).
Adjacency/evidence is provided for each step (the agent cites the presence of the next-page URL in each page's links list).

Minor issue: the adjacency verification text for the Computer science page is truncated in the provided output, so one small piece of evidence is not fully shown, but the agent still explicitly states the link and reached the target.

output.final_deliverable:
Chain of Wikipedia URLs (in order):

1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page
2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Contents
3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science
4. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science
5. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science

Notes on evidence for each adjacency (using only the provided visit results):

- Adjacency 1 -> 2 (Main_Page -> Portal:Contents): MISSING explicit link evidence. The Main Page visit result lists these links (sample):
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Contents/Portals
  (Main Page links shown in the visit result do not explicitly list https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Contents). See the Main Page visit record: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page

- Adjacency 2 -> 3 (Portal:Contents -> Portal:Science): MISSING explicit link evidence in the provided Portal:Contents links array. The Portal:Contents visit result's listed links (sample) do not explicitly show https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science. See the Portal:Contents visit record: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Contents

- Adjacency 3 -> 4 (Portal:Science -> Science): MISSING explicit link evidence in the provided Portal:Science links array. The Portal:Science visit result's listed links (sample) do not explicitly show https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science. See the Portal:Science visit record: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science

- Adjacency 4 -> 5 (Science -> Portal:Science): VERIFIED. The Science page's extracted links explicitly include https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science. Evidence from the Science visit result (links list includes):
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science
  Therefore: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science contains a link to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science. See the Science visit record: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science

Explicit verified adjacency pair (near the end):
- Verified: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science contains a link to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science
  Evidence (from provided visit result): the Science page 'links' list includes https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science

What is missing / caveats (per the provided evidence):
- The provided visit results do not explicitly show that Main Page links to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Contents.
- The provided Portal:Contents visit result (links array) does not explicitly show that it links to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science.
- The provided Portal:Science visit result (links array) does not explicitly show that it links to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science.

All URLs cited above come from the provided visit results (see these records):
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Contents
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science

If you want, I can (a) attempt to re-visit the pages and capture the full link lists to confirm the missing adjacencies, or (b) produce an alternate chain that only uses adjacencies explicitly shown in the provided link arrays.
output.action_summary: I built a 5-step chain using the visited pages in the merged results: Main_Page -> Portal:Contents -> Portal:Science -> Science -> Portal:Science. Using only the provided visit results, I could verify one adjacency near the end: the Science page links to the Portal:Science page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science contains a link to https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portal:Science). The other forward adjacencies (Main_Page -> Portal:Contents, Portal:Contents -> Portal:Science, Portal:Science -> Science) lack explicit link evidence in the supplied link lists; I noted those missing items and cited the relevant visited pages.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.
(

Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a Christian
neo-Byzantine style
influenced by ninth-century architecture, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

(March 2024)
This portal is
manually
maintained
.
This portal has a
single page layout
. It is possible that any
subpages
may no longer be needed.

take care

, and seek
consensus

.
Wikipedia portal for content related to Science
Science portal
Members of the Academy in 1667 with Louis XIV
Science
is a systematic discipline that builds and organises knowledge in the form of testable
hypotheses
and
predictions
about the universe. Modern science is typically divided into two – or three – major branches: the
natural sciences
, which study the
physical world
, and the
social sciences
, which study individuals and societies. While referred to as the
formal sciences
, the study of
logic
,
mathematics
, and
theoretical computer science
are typically regarded as separate because they rely on
deductive reasoning
instead of the
scientific method
as their main methodology. Meanwhile,
applied sciences
are disciplines that use scientific knowledge for practical purposes, such as engineering and medicine. (
Full article...
)
Outline of science
Refresh with new selections below (purge)
Featured article -
load new batch
Featured articles
are displayed here, which represent some of the best content on English Wikipedia.
Image 1
"Surveying a Dying Sun", cover of
If
, November 1953
The
Sun
has appeared as a
setting
in fiction at least since
classical antiquity
, but for a long time it received relatively sporadic attention. Many of the early depictions viewed it as an essentially
Earth-like
and thus potentially
habitable
body—a once-common belief about
celestial objects
in general known as the
plurality of worlds
—and depicted various kinds of solar inhabitants. As more became known about the Sun through advances in
astronomy
, in particular its temperature, solar inhabitants fell out of favour save for the occasional more exotic alien lifeforms. Instead, many stories focused on the eventual death of the Sun and the havoc it would wreak upon life on Earth. Before it was understood that the Sun is powered by
nuclear fusion
, the prevailing assumption among writers was that
combustion
was the source of its heat and light, and it was expected to run out of fuel relatively soon. Even after the true source of the Sun's energy was determined in the 1920s, the dimming or extinction of the Sun remained a recurring theme in
disaster stories
, with occasional attempts at averting disaster by reigniting the Sun. Another common way for the Sun to cause destruction is by exploding ("going
nova
"), and other mechanisms such as
solar flares
also appear on occasion. (
Full article...
)
Image 2
John Young
saluting the
United States flag
while jumping up on the Moon, with the
Apollo Lunar Module
Orion
and
Lunar Roving Vehicle
in the background
Apollo 16
(April 16–27, 1972) was the tenth
crewed
mission in the United States
Apollo space program
, administered by
NASA
, and the fifth and penultimate to
land on the Moon
. It was the second of Apollo's "
J missions
", with an extended stay on the
lunar surface
, a focus on science, and the use of the
Lunar Roving Vehicle
(LRV). The landing and
exploration
were in the
Descartes Highlands
, a site chosen because some scientists expected it to be an area formed by volcanic action, though this proved not to be the case. (
Full article...
)
Image 3
A CBC specimen in front of a printout displaying CBC and differential results
A
complete blood count
(
CBC
), also known as a
full blood count
(
FBC
) or
full haemogram
(
FHG
), is a set of
medical laboratory
tests that provide
information about the cells
in a person's
blood
. The CBC indicates the counts of
white blood cells
,
red blood cells
and
platelets
, the concentration of
hemoglobin
, and the
hematocrit
(the volume percentage of red blood cells). The
red blood cell indices
, which indicate the average size and hemoglobin content of red blood cells, are also reported, and a
white blood ce

Wikipedia portal for content related to Science
Science portal
Members of the Academy in 1667 with Louis XIV
Science
is a systematic discipline that builds and organises knowledge in the form of testable
hypotheses
and
predictions
about the universe. Modern science is typically divided into two – or three – major branches: the
natural sciences
, which study the
physical world
, and the
social sciences
, which study individuals and societies. While referred to as the
formal sciences
, the study of
logic
,
mathematics
, and
theoretical computer science
are typically regarded as separate because they rely on
deductive reasoning
instead of the
scientific method
as their main methodology. Meanwhile,
applied sciences
are disciplines that use scientific knowledge for practical purposes, such as engineering and medicine. (
Full article...
)
Outline of science
Refresh with new selections below (purge)
Featured article -
load new batch
Featured articles
are displayed here, which represent some of the best content on English Wikipedia.
Image 1
"Surveying a Dying Sun", cover of
If
, November 1953
The
Sun
has appeared as a
setting
in fiction at least since
classical antiquity
, but for a long time it received relatively sporadic attention. Many of the early depictions viewed it as an essentially
Earth-like
and thus potentially
habitable
body—a once-common belief about
celestial objects
in general known as the
plurality of worlds
—and depicted various kinds of solar inhabitants. As more became known about the Sun through advances in
astronomy
, in particular its temperature, solar inhabitants fell out of favour save for the occasional more exotic alien lifeforms. Instead, many stories focused on the eventual death of the Sun and the havoc it would wreak upon life on Earth. Before it was understood that the Sun is powered by
nuclear fusion
, the prevailing assumption among writers was that
combustion
was the source of its heat and light, and it was expected to run out of fuel relatively soon. Even after the true source of the Sun's energy was determined in the 1920s, the dimming or extinction of the Sun remained a recurring theme in
disaster stories
, with occasional attempts at averting disaster by reigniting the Sun. Another common way for the Sun to cause destruction is by exploding ("going
nova
"), and other mechanisms such as
solar flares
also appear on occasion. (
Full article...
)
Image 2
John Young
saluting the
United States flag
while jumping up on the Moon, with the
Apollo Lunar Module
Orion
and
Lunar Roving Vehicle
in the background
Apollo 16
(April 16–27, 1972) was the tenth
crewed
mission in the United States
Apollo space program
, administered by
NASA
, and the fifth and penultimate to
land on the Moon
. It was the second of Apollo's "
J missions
", with an extended stay on the
lunar surface
, a focus on science, and the use of the
Lunar Roving Vehicle
(LRV). The landing and
exploration
were in the
Descartes Highlands
, a site chosen because some scientists expected it to be an area formed by volcanic action, though this proved not to be the case. (
Full article...
)
Image 3
A CBC specimen in front of a printout displaying CBC and differential results
A
complete blood count
(
CBC
), also known as a
full blood count
(
FBC
) or
full haemogram
(
FHG
), is a set of
medical laboratory
tests that provide
information about the cells
in a person's
blood
. The CBC indicates the counts of
white blood cells
,
red blood cells
and
platelets
, the concentration of
hemoglobin
, and the
hematocrit
(the volume percentage of red blood cells). The
red blood cell indices
, which indicate the average size and hemoglobin content of red blood cells, are also reported, and a

Science
is a systematic discipline that builds and organises knowledge in the form of testable
hypotheses
and
predictions
about the universe.

Modern science is typically divided into two – or three – major branches:

3

natural sciences
, which study the
physical world
, and the
social sciences

[
4

While referred to as the
formal sciences
, the study of
logic
,
mathematics
, and
theoretical computer science
are typically regarded as separate because they rely on
deductive reasoning
instead of the
scientific method
as their main methodology.

Meanwhile,
applied sciences
are disciplines that use scientific knowledge for practical purposes, such as engineering and medicine.
[
10

11

[
12
]
The
history of science
spans the majority of the historical record, with the earliest identifiable predecessors to modern science dating to the
Bronze Age
in
Egypt
and
Mesopotamia
(
c.
3000–1200 BCE
). Their contributions to mathematics,
astronomy
, and
medicine
entered and shaped the Greek
natural philosophy
of
classical antiquity
and later
medieval scholarship
, whereby formal attempts were made to provide explanations of events in the
physical world
based on natural causes; while further advancements, including the introduction of the
Hindu–Arabic numeral system
, were made during the
Golden Age of India
and
Islamic Golden Age
.

Science
is a systematic discipline that builds and organises knowledge in the form of testable
hypotheses
and
predictions
about the universe.
[
1
]
: 49–71
[
2
]
Modern science is typically divided into two – or three – major branches:
[
3
]
the
natural sciences
, which study the
physical world
, and the
social sciences
, which study individuals and societies.
[
4
]
[
5
]
While referred to as the
formal sciences
, the study of
logic
,
mathematics
, and
theoretical computer science
are typically regarded as separate because they rely on
deductive reasoning
instead of the
scientific method
as their main methodology.
[
6
]
[
7
]
[
8
]
[
9
]
Meanwhile,
applied sciences
are disciplines that use scientific knowledge for practical purposes, such as engineering and medicine.
[
10
]
[
11
]
[
12
]
The
history of science
spans the majority of the historical record, with the earliest identifiable predecessors to modern science dating to the
Bronze Age
in
Egypt
and
Mesopotamia
(
c.
3000–1200 BCE
). Their contributions to mathematics,
astronomy
, and
medicine
entered and shaped the Greek
natural philosophy
of
classical antiquity
and later
medieval scholarship
, whereby formal attempts were made to provide explanations of events in the
physical world
based on natural causes; while further advancements, including the introduction of the
Hindu–Arabic numeral system
, were made during the
Golden Age of India
and
Islamic Golden Age
.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a Christian
neo-Byzantine style
influenced by ninth-century architecture, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

From today's featured article
First page of
The Detective's Album
Mary Fortune
(1832–1911) was an Australian writer who was one of the earliest female authors of
detective fiction
. Born in Ireland, she lived in Canada before moving to the Australian
goldfields
in 1855. She began writing for local newspapers soon after her arrival, and was a prolific pseudonymous contributor to
The Australian Journal
for more than four decades. Her writing primarily consisted of short crime stories – including her best-known work, the 500-story series
The Detective's Album
(pictured)
– but also included serial novels, journalism, poetry, and a memoir. She also wrote romances,
Gothic fiction
, and ghost stories. Her writing drew on her experiences in the goldfields and in Melbourne's rapidly urbanising environment; she often criticised colonial society and its treatment of women. Despite her popularity as a writer, Fortune experienced unstable housing and alcoholism, and died in poverty. Her identity, obscured by pseudonyms, was not rediscovered until the 1950s.

About
Today's featured picture
Westminster Cathedral
, officially the Metropolitan Cathedral of the Most Precious Blood, is a Catholic church at
Westminster
, London, England. It is the seat of the
Archbishop of Westminster
. Designed by
John Francis Bentley
in a Christian
neo-Byzantine style
influenced by ninth-century architecture, it is constructed almost entirely of brick, without steel reinforcements. The site on which the cathedral stands was purchased by the
Archdiocese of Westminster
in 1885 with construction taking place from 1895 to 1903. The cathedral has been visited by
Pope John Paul II
in 1982, by Queen
Elizabeth II
in 1995 – the first reigning monarch to attend a Catholic service since the
Reformation
– and by
Pope Benedict XVI
in 2010. This picture shows Westminster Cathedral's chapel of the Blessed Sacrament.

26ed365b-9eea-4893-a8b9-e4b788029cd3.title: Search for candidate links cited on the Main Page (to produce exact URLs for next visits)

details.intent: Return search snippets and URLs that reference or list important links from the Main Page; used as an auxiliary source to cross-check and produce exact URLs for subsequent visits.
target_facts: Search results containing exact Wikipedia URLs that appear on or are linked from the Main Page
Snippets that show anchor text or context so we can choose stable candidate links
verification_steps: Cross-check any candidate URLs from search results with links extracted directly from the Main Page visit (node 1) — prefer URLs present in the visit's _links_inline
If a URL appears in both sources, mark it as high-confidence for next visits
measurable_output_fields: high_confidence_urls: subset of search_urls present in visit node output
details.rationale: Although the Main Page visit will produce links, an independent search helps surface stable portal/article URLs and provides snippets we can use to justify selecting particular links for the chain. This reduces uncertainty about whether a candidate URL is indeed present on the Main Page.
details.goal: Search for candidate links cited on the Main Page (to produce exact URLs for next visits)
details.original_goal: Search for candidate links cited on the Main Page (to produce exact URLs for next visits)
details.parent_goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Return the chain in order, and include one verified adjacency pair near the end (show that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]).

action_result.intent: Return search snippets and URLs that reference or list important links from the Main Page; used as an auxiliary source to cross-check and produce exact URLs for subsequent visits.

244c99e8-f685-480b-9238-4cb683f1f134.title: Visit first linked Wikipedia page (chosen from Main Page links)

details.intent: Fetch the page content for the first hop in the chain (Portal:Contents). Extract its inline links so we can select the next page in the chain from its own outgoing links (satisfies the requirement that each next URL comes from links found on the previous page).
target_facts: All inline links on Portal:Contents (link_text, url)
Page title
Sections with collections of links (e.g., portals, main topics)
details.extraction_plan: Download Portal:Contents page content and parse _links_inline. Produce a ranked list of candidate URLs on that page to choose as the next hop (prefer article pages or topic portals).
measurable_output_fields: chosen_next_url: string (one URL selected from links_found for the next visit)
details.rationale: Portal:Contents is a likely stable hub linked from the Main Page and is a good source for the next hop; extracting its links gives verifiable exact URLs for the third node.
details.goal: Visit first linked Wikipedia page (chosen from Main Page links)
details.original_goal: Visit first linked Wikipedia page (chosen from Main Page links)
details.parent_goal: Start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Main_Page and build a chain of at least 4 Wikipedia URLs. Each next URL must come from links found on the previous visited page. Return the chain in order, and include one verified adjacency pair near the end (show that URL[i] links to URL[i+1]).

action_result.intent: Fetch the page content for the first hop in the chain (Portal:Contents). Extract its inline links so we can select the next page in the chain from its own outgoing links (satisfies the requirement that each next URL comes from links found on the previous page).

Wikipedia portal for content related to Science
Science portal
Members of the Academy in 1667 with Louis XIV
Science
is a systematic discipline that builds and organises knowledge in the form of testable
hypotheses
and
predictions
about the universe. Modern science is typically divided into two – or three – major branches: the
natural sciences
, which study the
physical world
, and the
social sciences
, which study individuals and societies. While referred to as the
formal sciences
, the study of
logic
,
mathematics
, and
theoretical computer science
are typically regarded as separate because they rely on
deductive reasoning
instead of the
scientific method
as their main methodology. Meanwhile,
applied sciences
are disciplines that use scientific knowledge for practical purposes, such as engineering and medicine. (
Full article...
)
Outline of science

Featured articles
are displayed here, which represent some of the best content on English Wikipedia.
Image 1
"Surveying a Dying Sun", cover of
If
, November 1953
The
Sun
has appeared as a
setting
in fiction at least since
classical antiquity
, but for a long time it received relatively sporadic attention. Many of the early depictions viewed it as an essentially
Earth-like
and thus potentially
habitable
body—a once-common belief about
celestial objects
in general known as the
plurality of worlds
—and depicted various kinds of solar inhabitants. As more became known about the Sun through advances in
astronomy
, in particular its temperature, solar inhabitants fell out of favour save for the occasional more exotic alien lifeforms. Instead, many stories focused on the eventual death of the Sun and the havoc it would wreak upon life on Earth. Before it was understood that the Sun is powered by
nuclear fusion
, the prevailing assumption among writers was that
combustion
was the source of its heat and light, and it was expected to run out of fuel relatively soon. Even after the true source of the Sun's energy was determined in the 1920s, the dimming or extinction of the Sun remained a recurring theme in
disaster stories
, with occasional attempts at averting disaster by reigniting the Sun. Another common way for the Sun to cause destruction is by exploding ("going
nova
"), and other mechanisms such as
solar flares
also appear on occasion. (
Full article...
)
Image 2
John Young
saluting the
United States flag
while jumping up on the Moon, with the
Apollo Lunar Module
Orion
and
Lunar Roving Vehicle
in the background
Apollo 16
(April 16–27, 1972) was the tenth
crewed
mission in the United States
Apollo space program
, administered by
NASA
, and the fifth and penultimate to
land on the Moon
. It was the second of Apollo's "
J missions
", with an extended stay on the
lunar surface
, a focus on science, and the use of the
Lunar Roving Vehicle
(LRV). The landing and
exploration
were in the
Descartes Highlands
, a site chosen because some scientists expected it to be an area formed by volcanic action, though this proved not to be the case. (
Full article...
)
Image 3
A CBC specimen in front of a printout displaying CBC and differential results
A
complete blood count
(
CBC
), also known as a
full blood count
(
FBC
) or
full haemogram
(
FHG
), is a set of
medical laboratory
tests that provide
information about the cells
in a person's
blood
. The CBC indicates the counts of
white blood cells
,
red blood cells
and
platelets
, the concentration of
hemoglobin
, and the
hematocrit
(the volume percentage of red blood cells). The
red blood cell indices
, which indicate the average size and hemoglobin content of red blood cells, are also reported, and a
white blood ce

v
t
e
Science
is a systematic discipline that builds and organises knowledge in the form of testable
hypotheses
and
predictions
about the universe.
[
1
]
: 49–71
[
2
]
Modern science is typically divided into two – or three – major branches:
[
3
]
the
natural sciences
, which study the
physical world
, and the
social sciences
, which study individuals and societies.
[
4
]
[
5
]
While referred to as the
formal sciences
, the study of
logic
,
mathematics
, and
theoretical computer science
are typically regarded as separate because they rely on
deductive reasoning
instead of the
scientific method
as their main methodology.
[
6
]
[
7
]
[
8
]
[
9
]
Meanwhile,
applied sciences
are disciplines that use scientific knowledge for practical purposes, such as engineering and medicine.
[
10
]
[
11
]
[
12
]
The
history of science
spans the majority of the historical record, with the earliest identifiable predecessors to modern science dating to the
Bronze Age
in
Egypt
and
Mesopotamia
(
c.
3000–1200 BCE
). Their contributions to mathematics,
astronomy
, and
medicine
entered and shaped the Greek
natural philosophy
of
classical antiquity
and later
medieval scholarship
, whereby formal attempts were made to provide explanations of events in the
physical world
based on natural causes; while further advancements, including the introduction of the
Hindu–Arabic numeral system
, were made during the
Golden Age of India
and
Islamic Golden Age
.
[
13
]
: 12
[
14
]
: 1–26
[
15
]
[
16
]
[
13
]
: 163–192
The recovery and assimilation of
Greek works
and
Islamic inquiries
into Western Europe during the
Renaissance
re
